summary: A symmetrical three-storey rectangular apartment-like building with a central raised corridor/tower running along the long axis and small corner projections. Constructed from alternating horizontal bands (light yellow banding and darker stone-looking bands), small regular windows, a brick-tile roof ridge and chimney, and a low decorative perimeter of white blocks enclosing a dirt courtyard.

shape.overall_form: rectangular main mass with central raised spine/tower and small corner projections
shape.symmetry: bilaterally symmetric along the long central axis

materials.name: stone_slab
materials.role: horizontal banding (gray slabs) and roof edging

elements: regular small horizontal windows (repeating on each floor)
central raised corridor/tower running along roof ridge with chimney
three floors with horizontal banding between floors
corner vertical pilasters/posts in white material
enclosing low white block perimeter and dirt courtyard
rebuild_hints: Create a rectangular 3-floor volume with repeating horizontal bands: light band for wall, thin darker stone band between floors and at base.
Place small 1x1 or 2x1 windows in a regular grid across each band, aligned horizontally.
Add a central raised spine/tower along the long axis: a raised walkway/roof ridge with brick tiles and a chimney stack near center-back.
Add white vertical corner/posts (quartz or similar) at the building corners and at mid-courtyard entrances; surround site with a single-layer white block perimeter on a dirt ground.
Roof uses brick slabs/blocks forming a low gabled ridge with small stepped crenellations near top.
uncertainties: Exact block types: light wall appears to be sandstone but could be sand or another light textured block.
Exact dimensions: width/depth/height are estimated from pixel views; actual block counts may differ by several blocks.
Internal layout and door positions are not visible; entrance location and floor access are assumed along the central spine but uncertain.
Roof slope detail (true gable angle) and slab vs full block usage is partially obscured; described as gable based on visible ridge.
